113:
China's embassy in Manila filed a diplomatic protest because Jay Tarriela used a cartoon of Xi Jinping in a talk and posted it online. The slide said, "Why China remains to be a bully?" with Xi drawn in silly, angry poses.

Beijing called that a "serious violation of China's political dignity" and a "blatant political provocation" that "crossed the red line." They sent formal messages to Malacañang, DFA, and the Coast Guard, demanding to know if this reflects official policy and why Tarriela has not been punished.

All of that. Over a cartoon.

If you cannot stand satire, you have no business demanding respect from the very country your ships are harassing. If you cannot accept that Filipinos will call out your bullying, you have no business being in Philippine waters at all.

🟥 WHILE THEY DO THIS IN OUR WATERS

Now put that beside what China is actually doing in the West Philippine Sea.

A Filipino fishing boat, Prince LJ, was harassed near Bajo de Masinloc this January. A China Coast Guard vessel and a PLA Navy ship came as close as about 30 meters, blared sirens, and blocked its path for around three hours until the Philippine Coast Guard arrived to help.

At Second Thomas Shoal, Chinese coast guard ships have used high-pressure water cannons on Philippine resupply boats, smashing windows, damaging ships, and injuring sailors on the Unaizah May 4.

Chinese ships have aimed lasers at Coast Guard crews and used dangerous maneuvers. Chinese personnel have boarded Philippine boats, attacked marines, and seized equipment during resupply missions.

This is happening in areas where the 2016 arbitral ruling already said China has no historic rights and where our rights are clear.

But the thing that "crossed the red line" was a PowerPoint slide.

🟥 THE WEST PHILIPPINE SEA IS OURS

This part is important and it needs to be said straight.

The West Philippine Sea is not "disputed" just because China says so. The arbitral award in 2016 did not sit on the fence. It said China's sweeping claim has no legal basis and upheld our rights over our exclusive economic zone and continental shelf.

When we casually call it "disputed waters," we repeat their framing. We make it sound like both sides have equal legal claims. They do not.

To say "it is disputed" is to quietly step back from what the ruling already recognized. It is to blur the line between what is ours and what China wants to grab.

The West Philippine Sea is ours.

Those seas off Zambales and Palawan are ours. The fishing grounds our grandparents worked are ours. The waters around Ayungin and Bajo de Masinloc are ours. We are not intruding. We are not "provoking." We are at home.

China is the one intruding. China is the one forcing fishermen to turn back. China is the one blocking resupply. China is the one trying to erase a ruling it did not like.

🟥 THE BULLY THAT CANNOT TAKE A JOKE

China lost a landmark case in The Hague. It refuses to accept it, keeps sending its coast guard and militia, and keeps harassing anyone under a Philippine flag in those waters.

Then one Filipino official uses a meme to call them a bully. Beijing's reaction:

Dramatic language about "political dignity."  
Talk of "red lines" and "countermeasures."  
Pressure on our government to control one man's words.

Tarriela is right when he says this is an attempt to intimidate him and distract from China's aggressive and illegal actions in the West Philippine Sea. If one cartoon shakes them this much, that is not power. That is paranoia.

Leaders who cannot take criticism should not be leaders. Governments that cannot survive a cartoon without panicking do not deserve the respect they keep demanding.

🟥 WHAT POLITICAL DIGNITY ARE YOU TALKING ABOUT?

China keeps throwing around this phrase: "serious violation of China's political dignity."

What dignity? Where?

Dignity comes from conduct. It comes from following the rules, respecting others, and acting with integrity. China does none of those things in the West Philippine Sea.

China lost in international court. China ignored the verdict. China keeps sending armed ships to blockade Philippine territory. China harasses fishermen. China shoots lasers and water cannons at our vessels. China boards our boats and beats up our Marines.

That is not dignity. That is a crime spree with diplomatic language slapped on top.

You do not get to commit theft and then claim hurt feelings when someone calls you a thief. You do not get to assault people and then demand they apologize for your "reputation." You do not get to violate international law and then cry foul because a meme hurt your image.

Dignity is earned. It is not granted by diplomatic courtesy or by silencing critics. And it is not something you can demand while your actions destroy it every single day.

If China wants political dignity, it should start by acting with honor. Until then, all the formal protests in the world will not change what everyone already sees: a bully with thin skin trying to hide behind big words.

🟥 SATIRE IS SPEECH. WATER CANNONS ARE NOT.

Satire is speech.  
Cartoons are speech.  
Memes are speech.

Water cannons smashing into a wooden hull are not speech. Lasers aimed at a bridge crew are not speech. Boarding a boat and roughing up marines is not speech.

China wants it flipped. It wants its use of force to be treated as normal "law enforcement," and our words to be treated as dangerous "provocation." It wants to bully us at sea and then scold us for being rude online.

That is not how this works. That is not how any of this works.

🟥 GET OUT OF OUR SEAS

China can call memes a "serious violation," "smear," or "red line." The facts will still be the same.

The arbitral award stripped their nine-dash line of any legal basis.  
Our fishers and our sailors are the ones getting hit and blocked.  
Their ships are the ones acting like they own waters that are not theirs.

If Beijing wants less anger from Filipinos, it is simple: stop the harassment.  
Pull your coast guard and PLA ships out of our exclusive economic zone.  
Stop chasing, blocking, and injuring our people.

Until then, we will keep talking. We will keep posting. We will keep drawing cartoons. We will keep saying the truth:

The West Philippine Sea is ours.

This blog is inspired by a post from La Vérité  about patronage politics, which Ma'am Heidi Mendoza shared. 

It pulled me straight into thinking about her 2025 Senate run and what it quietly says about where we are, and where we might be headed.

On the surface, her story is simple. She ran. She finished 21st. End of story.

But if you stop there, you are missing the most important part.

She ran with almost no money, no political clan behind her, and no traditional machinery. 

Her camp says she spent around 2.5 million pesos for the whole campaign. 

In the end, about 8.7 million people still shaded her name on their ballots.

Now put that beside the people who did win.

Comelec records and campaign finance reports show names like Camille Villar declaring around 179.6 million pesos in spending. DDS-aligned and administration-friendly candidates like Bong Go, Bato dela Rosa, Erwin Tulfo, Bong Revilla, Benhur Abalos, and Rodante Marcoleta all reported spending in the tens and hundreds of millions. 

Some of them, plus their allies, together poured over a billion pesos into TV and radio ads even before the official campaign period.

Heidi: 2.5 million.  

Them: 100 million, 150 million, 170 million, plus months of billion-peso ad buys.

Heidi: roughly 25 centavos per vote.  

Them: many pesos per vote, once you count ads, ground operatives, allowances, “logistics.”

That gap already tells you what game she refused to play.

Her campaign looked different once you zoom in. 

No plastic bags with a family logo and a kilo of rice inside.

No coordinators lining up for envelopes. 

No ordered “solid vote” from barangay captains. 

Her “machinery” was volunteers. People who discovered her on interviews and on social media. 

Supporters who printed their own tarpaulins from files they downloaded. 

A tricycle driver who handed over 40 pesos and told them to use it for campaign materials.

She lived on free interviews and long Q&As.

She sat on ANC and other shows explaining where the money goes, what she uncovered in the Armed Forces corruption cases, what she saw in Makati, why the national budget matters for ordinary households. 

No jingle. No dance number. No promise of instant ayuda.

Right before election day, surveys had her at around 5.8 percent. 

When the results came in, she ended at about 15.7 percent. 

That almost 10-point jump was the biggest difference between polling and actual votes in that race. Pollsters did not see her surge coming.

So yes, she did not enter the magic 12. But if the only thing we say is “she lost,” we are being lazy. 

That 8.7 million is a signal; a proof that millions of Filipinos will vote for someone who refuses to play the patronage game, even when that person has no dynasty and almost no budget.

For a system built on patronage, that is not nothing. That is a crack in the wall.

🟥 WHO REALLY RUNS THE COUNTRY

Step back and look at who sits in power all over the country.

Governors, mayors, representatives: you see the same surnames cycling through different positions. 

Father is governor, mother is mayor, son is in Congress, cousin is in the city council. 

Term limits just mean they switch seats. The family never leaves.

In some provinces, one clan holds so many posts at once that people now call them “fat dynasties.” 

In others, you can list five, ten, almost twenty relatives in government in a single cycle. 

Marcos in the north. 
Duterte in the south. 
Singson in the Ilocos. 
Villars in the Senate and local posts. 
Estrada and Ejercito scattered across different positions. 
Binay in Makati and beyond.

When people map this out against poverty, the pattern is ugly. 

Many of the poorest provinces have been under dynastic control for years. 

The family photos change as children and grandchildren step in. 

The poverty rates barely move.

Economists like Ronald Mendoza have shown this in numbers: poverty and dynasties feed each other. 

Poor areas are fertile ground for dynasties. Dynasties keep the system in place that allows poverty to linger. It is a loop.

So Heidi’s 8.7 million votes did not land on a clean slate. 

They landed on a board that is already heavily stacked in favor of clans who own the ground game, the air war, and the money.

🟥 HOW IT WORKS IN THE BARANGAY

If you have ever watched a local campaign up close, you know how patronage really works.

A national candidate rarely presses the money directly into a voter’s hand. 

There is always a chain. 

At the bottom are the barangay captains, kagawads, and local “coordinators.” 

These are the people who know which families are desperate for cash, who needs a job, who has a sick parent in the hospital, who can be asked to attend a rally in exchange for a food pack.

Studies done in cities like Iloilo and other areas say the same thing: barangay officials themselves believe patronage is the most reliable way to win. 

It is the main strategy, not an add-on. 

They see giving out aid, jobs, and small projects as part of their political work, not a violation.

Government programs like cash assistance, emergency ayuda, and short-term work schemes are supposed to be apolitical. 

On paper, they are. 

In real life, election reports, COMELEC cases, and news stories keep catching the same pattern: distribution suddenly spikes close to election time, lists are filtered through local leaders, and people are gently or openly reminded whom they should be grateful to when they vote.

Big projects scale this up. 

Look at the flood control mess. Billions allocated to protect communities from floods. 

But a huge slice of that money ends up in questionable contracts with a small circle of contractors. 

Ghost projects. Overpriced works. 

A cut for the politician who inserted the item, a cut for the public works officials who sign off, a cut for whoever keeps the audit quiet.

Those projects, when they exist, become photo ops and talking points.

“We built this for you.” 

When they don’t, the money still moves, just under the surface, and still strengthens the same political networks.

This is what Heidi ran against. 

Not just individuals, but an entire way of doing politics where favors and funds flow from the top down, and loyalty flows from the bottom up.

🟥 HEIDI AS A TEST CASE

Seen from that angle, her campaign is not a sad story of a reformer who failed. 

It is a live test case of what happens when someone tries to stand outside patronage.

No dynasty. 
No war chest. 
No block voting from religious groups. 
No control over government programs. 
No “solid north” or “solid south.”

Just a record as a state auditor who followed the money in places where she was not supposed to look: multimillion plunder in the Armed Forces, overpriced buildings in Makati, patterns of waste and theft in budget use.

Her platform was not built around giveaways. 

It was built around ideas most politicians avoid because they sound boring or technical: budget literacy, transparent audits, abolishing pork. 

She wanted people to be able to see, line by line, where funds go, and to be able to push back when they do not agree.

Her volunteers were not being paid per head. 

They were students, professionals, retirees, people who discovered her through old footage of her testifying in Congress, or new interviews online.

They printed their own flyers. 

They used their own fuel. 

They spent their own weekends canvassing.

And even with all that, she almost never led the surveys. 

She broke into the middle of the pack late and then shocked everyone with her final number.

All of that is important, because it puts a shape and a price tag on anti-patronage politics under current conditions. 

It says: if you refuse the usual tricks, expect a ceiling. 

But it also says: the ceiling is not as low as the machine wants you to believe.

🟥 WHY THE SYSTEM STILL HOLDS

If this desire for something different is already there, why is patronage still so strong?

Part of it is written into the rules.

The Constitution says the state shall prohibit political dynasties “as may be defined by law.” That sounds firm until you remember who has to write that law. 

Congress. A place filled with members of the same political families we are talking about.

Every time an anti-dynasty bill comes up, it runs into the same wall. 

Lawmakers will water it down, delay it, or quietly kill it in committee. 

They will support mild versions that look good on paper but are easy for big clans to work around. 

Why would they willingly pass a law that tells their own children, siblings, and spouses they cannot run?

The courts have been asked to step in. Petitions have asked the Supreme Court to force Congress to enact an anti-dynasty law. 

The Court has always declined, citing separation of powers. 

So we are left with a promise in the Constitution with no teeth.

The party system does not help either. Our parties are not strong organizations with clear beliefs. 

They are loose alliances built around whoever is powerful at the moment. 

After every presidential election, you see the migration. 

Politicians jump ship to the winning side. 

Old enemies become new allies overnight if it means getting closer to the budget and to appointments.

In that environment, surname and resources become the real party. 

People do not vote for “Party X’s program.” They vote for the name that has delivered visible benefits to them, or the name they see everywhere on TV and online.

Then there is poverty. 

People who are struggling day to day cannot afford to treat elections as pure moral exercises. 

When a candidate offers money, a job, or urgent help with a bill, it is hard to say no if there is no guarantee that anyone else will help.

Interviews with voters in poor communities keep bringing up the same theme: accepting money or goods from a politician is seen as a practical move, not a betrayal of democracy. 

For many, the vote is secret anyway. 

They will take what is offered, then vote their conscience—or stick with the patron if they genuinely feel indebted.

As long as the state itself does not guarantee basic security and services, patrons will fill that gap. And every time they do, they strengthen the cycle.

🟥 THE OTHER SIDE OF THE STORY: VOTERS ARE ALREADY MOVING

Here is the part that gives some hope, and it is important to say it without sugarcoating anything.

Heidi is not the only sign that Filipinos are willing to support candidates who are openly against patronage and abuse of power.

Look at the 2025 midterms. 

Kiko Pangilinan and Bam Aquino both returned to the Senate after being written off by some analysts. 

These are not candidates known for handing out envelopes or building political clans. 

They are known for work on food security, education, reform, and standing up to previous administrations. 

Their comeback was driven by people who remember their record and wanted them back, not by some huge new patronage machine.

Chel Diokno, after two failed Senate runs, finally made it into Congress as the first nominee of Akbayan party-list. 

Akbayan topped the party-list race and got the full three seats. 

A big slice of that support came from young voters who have been following Chel for years, not because he had money to spend on them, but because he has been consistent on rule of law and human rights.

Leila de Lima, who spent years in detention on charges many see as politically motivated, also won a seat as a party-list representative. 

After everything that was thrown at her, enough people still believed in what she stood for to send her back into formal politics.

Put those together with Heidi’s 8.7 million votes. You start to see a shape: a sizable bloc of voters who are willing to support people identified with accountability, good governance, and human rights, even when these candidates do not have dominant machines or control over public funds. 

It is not yet a majority. It is not yet fully organized. But it exists.

🟥 WHY 2028 MATTERS

So where does that leave us?

Patronage politics is still the default setting. Dynasties still hold most of the levers. Money still talks loudly. 

The justice system still struggles to punish powerful people consistently. 

On that front, nothing in this blog is optimistic.

But underneath that, you can feel something shifting.

Eight point seven million people voted for an auditor with no machinery. 

Kiko and Bam walked back into the Senate after everyone had written their political obituaries. 

Chel finally crossed the line. Leila came back from a jail cell into the House. These are not the results of a country that is hopelessly in love with trapo politics.

The desire to move away from patronage is already here. It is scattered. It is uneven. It is not always sure of itself. But it is here.

What is missing is coordination.

In every election, people like you and me sit with our ballots and try to vote “the right way” alone. We choose twelve names based on conscience, records, and gut feel. 

We split our votes across too many people. Reform votes get scattered. Traditional machines stay solid.

Heidi’s numbers show what happens when that desire is strong but scattered. 

You get millions for one candidate, millions for another, millions more for others who share the same basic fight. 

Then you wake up to see the top twelve still crowded with the same surnames and their allies.

If those votes moved in a more coordinated way, some walls would crack faster.

2028 is the next big test. 

That is not far away. The system will be ready. 

Dynasties are already planning, grooming the next generation, building new alliances, testing new faces they can sell to us.

The question is whether the rest of us will still be behaving like we are alone, or whether we will finally act like we understand the weight of our collective vote.

Heidi’s campaign, and the wins of people like Pangilinan, Aquino, Diokno, and de Lima, are not accidents. 

They are early signs. They say: the ground is moving, but the old house is still standing.

If we do nothing, the system will absorb those shocks, repaint the same walls, and tell us it is “change.” 

If we get our heads together, support fewer names more strongly, and treat our vote as something we use together instead of separately, then those cracks can become breaks.

Patronage politics is not going away tomorrow. It is too deep, too convenient for those who benefit. But the idea that we are stuck with it forever is also a lie.

We already see, in numbers and in names, that something else is possible. 

The question for 2028 is simple and brutal: will we act like we believe that, or will we shrug, take what is handed to us, and let the same machine run our lives for another generation?

🟥 WHO ROLLS OUT THE WHEELCHAIR FIRST?  

Plunder is now a genre in Philippine politics. Same cast, updated script, bigger budget.

Right now, the flood control scandal is that script. 

Billions were supposedly poured into climate and flood projects, but people are still wading through brown water to get to work. 

And as soon as investigations start sounding serious, everyone’s bracing for the usual scene: the wheelchair, the neck brace, the “delikado sa health ko ang detention” routine.

This time, it feels heavier. Because the money involved was meant to keep people’s homes, sari-sari stores, and roads above water.

🟥 THE US TRIP THAT WON’T END (BONOAN)  

Former DPWH Secretary Manuel Bonoan officially left for the US in November 2025 to “assist his wife” with a medical procedure. 

On paper, that sounds normal. The problem is what happened next.

He said he would be back by December 17.

He didn’t come back. 

By mid-January, the Ombudsman was already saying he was overstaying in the US and could be deported if his visa extension wasn’t validated.

At the same time:  

- He’s on an immigration lookout bulletin linked to flood control anomalies.  

- The Senate’s Blue Ribbon committee issued a subpoena for him to appear in their hearing on the flood control mess.  

- Senators are now talking about possibly withdrawing that subpoena if his plea to stay in the US for “medical reasons” is validated.

So instead of explaining where the money went and why ghost projects exist, the conversation turns into: is his medical excuse valid, should he be allowed to stay abroad a bit longer, is it fair to make him fly home?

He hasn’t shown up in a wheelchair yet, but the pattern is familiar: start with a family medical reason, stay abroad longer than promised, use that situation to neutralize or delay pressure from hearings.

If he eventually returns and we suddenly see airport footage of a frail, escorted Bonoan in a mask and wheelchair, it won’t feel surprising. It will feel like the continuation of something that already started months ago.

🟥 THE SEQUEL KINGS: REVILLA AND ESTRADA  

Senator Jinggoy Estrada and former senator Bong Revilla Jr. are front and center in this scandal.

The justice department has filed plunder complaints against them tied to flood control projects. 

They’re accused, based on sworn statements from DPWH insiders, of receiving kickbacks from contracts that were either overpriced, substandard, or completely fake. 

In one case, former officials say hundreds of millions were delivered to Revilla’s Cavite residence and before the 2025 elections. 

Both men deny everything and say the accusations are politically motivated.

The Ombudsman has also filed graft and malversation charges against Revilla over a ghost flood control project in Bulacan worth around 90‑plus million. 

These are cases where some charges are non‑bailable once warrants are issued. That’s when the pressure usually spikes.

The important detail: both Estrada and Revilla have seen this movie before. 

During the pork barrel era, they learned exactly how slow the system moves, how public opinion can be managed, and how illness—real or claimed—can soften the narrative and buy time.

They know what a hospital admission or wheelchair appearance can do for them:  

- Delay court proceedings.  
- Shift media coverage from “plunder” to “health scare.”  
- Invite sympathy from supporters who already feel their idols are being persecuted.

So if one day soon, just as an arrest warrant is about to be served or a court appearance is non‑negotiable, news breaks that one of them has been rushed to the hospital or advised not to travel, it won’t feel random. 

It will look like a strategy they’ve already road‑tested in the past.

🟥 THE FUGITIVE DOING THE LONG MEDICAL ANGLE (ZALDY CO)  

Then there’s former Ako Bicol Rep. Zaldy Co.

He’s also implicated in the flood control mess, accused of benefiting from the way projects and contractors were steered. 

Instead of showing up to face this, he left the Philippines in 2025, citing medical treatment abroad. 

After that, the trail gets longer: talk of him possibly staying in Portugal, his passport being cancelled, luxury vehicles seized back home, his lawyers warning of lawsuits.

He’s not in a wheelchair in a court hallway. He’s somewhere outside the country, using distance plus a medical story to justify why he’s not in any hearing room at all.

That’s a different version of the same instinct. Instead of collapsing on a stretcher at Sandiganbayan, you disappear into a foreign clinic and try to stretch that status for as long as possible.

In his case, the question isn’t “when does he roll into a hearing in a wheelchair” but “does he ever intend to physically appear in a Philippine court if he can stretch the medical narrative from abroad?”

🟥 THE FLOOD ANGLE  

Flood control corruption is not a side story. This is money tagged as climate and disaster funding. It’s supposed to pay for drainage, river improvements, dikes, pumping stations—the boring, unglamorous infrastructure that keeps people’s feet dry when the rain hits hard.

Investigations and reports describe some ugly patterns:  

- A small cluster of contractors cornering a huge chunk of flood projects.  
- Contracts awarded for projects that residents say were never fully built, or exist mostly on paper.  
- Climate funds and public works budgets treated as an ATM for operators, middlemen, and politicians.

Every “ghost” desilting, every substandard floodwall means:  

- A street that floods faster.  
- A family that has to move their appliances to higher ground every year.  
- A child who can’t go to school because the route is waist‑deep in dirty water.

So when the names tied to this kind of plunder start reaching for medical excuses the moment accountability gets serious, it crosses a line. 

It’s not just technical graft. It’s a direct line from signatures on paper to muddy water in people’s homes.

🟥 SO WHO GOES HOSPITAL FIRST?  

If you look at behavior, risk, and resources:

- Bonoan is already playing around the edges of the hospitalization claim. He’s abroad for a medical‑related reason, missed his original return date, and now everything hangs on whether his extended stay is medically justified. He’s the closest to formally turning health into a shield.

- Revilla and Estrada are positioned for the classic version. High‑profile, used to legal battles, facing serious cases where detention is a real possibility. Once those cases move into a phase where arrest and tighter court schedules are on the table, the incentive for sudden health issues grows.

Whoever moves first won’t be innovating. They’ll just be using a play this country has allowed for years: when the law starts to close in, the body suddenly becomes the last line of defense.

I got curious the moment I heard the line: a lawyer telling his client, “don’t surrender yet, we’ll exhaust all legal remedies first.”

I’m not a lawyer. My instinct when I hear statements like that is to read, research, and fact-check. I want to know if the words match how the law actually works, or if they just sound good on air.

Here is what I found, for anyone else who’s curious. If any of this reads wrong to you, feel free to correct me.

🟥 THE ANG CASE IN PLAIN VIEW

In January 2026, the Regional Trial Court in Sta. Cruz, Laguna issued warrants of arrest against Charlie “Atong” Ang and 17 others in connection with the missing sabungeros case, on serious, non-bailable charges like kidnapping with homicide and kidnapping with serious illegal detention. 

Within days, authorities arrested or took into custody the co-accused. Ang is the one still out.

His lawyer, Atty. Gabriel Villareal, went on air and said he advised Ang not to surrender yet, and that they would first “exhaust all available legal remedies” before he turns himself in. 

On the surface, that sounds like legal strategy: check if the warrant is valid, question the process, file something before walking into a cell.

But in reality, government agencies have since called Ang a fugitive from justice, put up a ₱10 million reward for information leading to his arrest, canceled his firearm licenses, and launched manhunts. 

That gap between the polished line and the actual consequences is what pushed me to look for what the rules actually say.

🟥 THE OLD CASE WHERE A LAWYER CROSSED THE LINE

I looked for a case where the Supreme Court dealt with a lawyer telling a client not to face an arrest warrant. One decision stood out.

In Maria Cristina Zabaljauregui Pitcher vs. Atty. Rustico B. Gagate (A.C. No. 9532, October 7, 2013), Pitcher had been charged with grave coercion in Criminal Case No. 337985 before the Metropolitan Trial Court of Makati City, Branch 63, after a dispute where locks on an office were changed. 

When the court issued a warrant of arrest, her lawyer, Atty. Gagate, told her to go into hiding until he had filed the necessary motions. He then basically abandoned her case.

The Supreme Court called that out very clearly. It said advising a client to go into hiding in order to evade arrest is not proper legal advice and is a transgression of the ordinary processes of law. 

It found that this violated his duty to use only fair and honest means to pursue a client’s lawful objectives, and it suspended him from the practice of law for three years, ordering him to return the ₱150,000 acceptance fee.

That decision sets a hard boundary: when advice crosses over into “go hide so they can’t arrest you,” the Court treats that as an ethical violation.

🟥 THE GRAY AREA WHERE ANG’S LAWYER STANDS

Real situations are rarely as clean as “surrender now” versus “run away.”

A lawyer is supposed to:

- explain the charges and possible penalties,  
- lay out the legal remedies to attack a warrant or an information,  
- walk through what happens if you surrender,  
- and what happens if you do not.

The line they cannot cross is becoming a partner in avoiding the law. 

There is a difference between describing all the options, including risky ones, and actively endorsing “evade that warrant.”

In public, Atty. Villareal’s framing is that the warrant against Ang is premature and legally questionable, that the court didn’t fully consider their side, and that they plan to challenge it through proper motions and petitions. 

In that light, “do not surrender yet” is packaged as rights talk: fix the legal defects first, then submit.

The problem is that the legal environment around fugitives makes that middle ground much shakier than it sounds.

🟥 FUGITIVES, REMEDIES, AND WHY SURRENDER MATTERS

Recent Supreme Court guidance has leaned into a simple idea: if you are a fugitive from justice, you cannot expect the courts to entertain your requests in the same way as someone who submits to their jurisdiction. 

You cannot ignore a warrant and at the same time ask that same system to grant you relief. 

You surrender first, then you are heard. Voluntary surrender is what restores full standing.

Under that approach, a person who knows there is a warrant and still stays out can be treated as a fugitive and lose standing to seek certain remedies. 

It is not enough that their lawyer is filing things on their behalf. 

The courts can simply say: until you show up, you do not get to ask us for favors.

Put that beside a line like “we will exhaust all legal remedies before surrendering.”

If the system is already treating you as a fugitive while you are “exhausting remedies,” many of those remedies are effectively dead from the start. 

The documents might be moving, but your position is weaker because you are not submitting to the arrest.

On the other side of the equation is voluntary surrender. Under Article 13(7) of the Revised Penal Code, voluntary surrender is a mitigating circumstance that can lower the penalty if you are convicted. 

For it to count, you must not yet have been arrested, you have to surrender to a person in authority or their agent, and it has to look like a genuine decision to submit.

Beyond sentencing, surrender has practical effects:

- it avoids the “fugitive from justice” label,  
- it can help when asking for bail where bail is allowed,  
- and it presents you as someone willing to face the process, which judges do notice.

Set that against Ang’s current reality: declared a fugitive, facing a ₱10 million bounty, the subject of manhunts. Whatever the strengths of his defense, it is hard to say this posture gives him any legal edge.

🟥 SO, DID THE LAWYER’S ADVICE GO TOO FAR

As an outsider, there is a hard limit: we do not know the exact words used in private between Ang and Atty. Villareal. 

What we have is the public version: do not surrender yet, exhaust legal remedies first, we respect the courts.

If the private advice was essentially “go into hiding, stay out of sight while we try to keep you free,” that starts to look very close to what Atty. Gagate did in 2013, which the Supreme Court has already condemned and punished.

If the private advice was more like “here are all the options, but my recommendation is that you surrender and we fight this from inside the process,” that would be another story.

From what is visible, though, the timeline looks like this:

- January 2026: the Sta. Cruz, Laguna RTC issues warrants of arrest against Ang and 17 others in the missing sabungeros case.  

- Within days, 17 co-accused are arrested or in custody; Ang alone remains at large.  

- Atty. Villareal publicly says he advised Ang not to surrender yet while they exhaust legal remedies.  

- The DOJ and DILG brand Ang a fugitive from justice and offer a ₱10 million reward for his arrest.

That pattern feels less like a careful use of legal remedies and more like a dance around a lawful court order that is being sold as strategy. Even as a non-lawyer, it is hard not to see the risk in that.

🟥 WHAT STRONGER, SAFER ADVICE COULD LOOK LIKE

After going through all this, the version of “good” or at least safer advice in my head is simple.

A lawyer in this situation could:

- explain the charges and likely penalties honestly,  
- walk through the strength and gaps of the known evidence,  
- lay out the real-world effect of all the legal remedies: motions, petitions, bail, questioning probable cause,  
- make it clear that staying out with an active warrant can lead to fugitive status and weaken or kill some remedies,  
- recommend voluntary surrender, and personally help arrange it in a safe, coordinated way.

Then the full fight happens from inside the legal process: motions to quash, challenges to the warrant, attacks on the evidence, bail hearings. 

The lawyer still fights. The client’s rights are still asserted. But nobody is pretending that ignoring a warrant is neutral.

That balance—fight hard, but not by telling your client to sidestep a lawful order—is what feels both ethical and effective.

🟥 WHAT STAYS WITH ME

The more I learned about this issue, the less my core question was “is this technically allowed” and the more it became “who really benefits from this advice.”

On paper, “we will exhaust all legal remedies before surrendering” sounds strong and protective. 

In practice, the picture right now is:

- a fugitive label,  
- a ₱10 million reward,  
- an active manhunt,  
- and a weaker legal position than if there had been an early, coordinated surrender paired with a serious legal challenge.

If the trend in the courts is to limit what fugitives can ask for, then telling someone “stay out while we file” starts to look like putting them in a worse position and calling it strategy.

People go to lawyers believing they will be guided closer to solid legal ground. 

When the advice leaves them more exposed, with fewer remedies and more heat, it is fair to ask whether that advice really served the client, or just served an image of defiance in the media.

So, is it always a legal violation when a lawyer tells a client not to surrender?

From what I’ve read, not automatically. 

But the more that advice looks like a way to dodge a valid court order instead of facing it and challenging it, the closer it moves to something the Supreme Court has already punished before.

117:
Binary thinking is the laziest upgrade we’ve given to our politics.

You’re either kakampink or DDS. 
Pro or anti. 
For this family or that family. 
One color or the other. 

The moment you say something slightly complicated, people don’t ask what you mean. 

They ask: “So which side are you on?”

That question feels normal now, but it’s already a trap. 

A lot of what we’re living in has a simple name: FALSE DICHOTOMY. 

It means you are told there are only two options, when in reality there are more. 

“You’re either with us or against us” is the standard line. It’s not just bad logic. It’s a way to force you to choose a team and stop thinking.

🟥 WHEN “TWO SIDES” SHRINKS YOUR BRAIN

Take the drug war.

You can believe drugs seriously harm communities, and still be horrified by people getting killed without trial. 

You can support strong law enforcement, and still insist police must follow the law. That is what basic, adult thinking looks like.

Our public conversation doesn’t like that. 

You either “support the drug war” or you’re “anti-Duterte.” 

Once that frame sets in, any middle position looks suspicious or cowardly. 

Studies of Duterte’s speeches show this pattern clearly: the country is described as a battle between brave supporters and bad elements blocking the way.

That is the core problem with false choices. They make layered thinking and reasoning look fake, weak, or traitorous. They don’t just narrow debate. They punish thought.

Disagreement itself is not the issue. Democracy needs clear differences. Voters should see real choices. Some level of taking sides is normal and even useful. 

The danger starts when every issue, every person, every news item is squeezed into a hard “us versus them” frame, with no room in between.

🟥 WHY OUR MINDS LOVE SIMPLE STORIES

Part of this is just how the mind works.

It is easier to think in black-and-white. It is easier to say “good guys, bad guys” than to hold a messy picture in your head. 

Psychologists describe this as a shortcut: instead of weighing lots of details, the brain jumps to “yes or no,” “team A or team B.”

Sometimes that is helpful. If your house is on fire, you don’t need critical thinking. You need the exit.

Most political problems are not fire alarms. 
They are slow, layered, complicated. 

Poverty, crime, education, health care, corruption, foreign policy—each has many causes and moving parts. 

Treat them like simple either/or questions and you guarantee bad answers.

Research on rigid thinking shows that people who are mentally inflexible cling to one story and reject any new information that doesn’t fit. 

That is being stuck. 

Once “my side is always right” becomes your base setting, every new fact has only one job: prove your side right again.

At that point, you're just defending. You're  not thinking  anymore. 

🟥 OUR FAVORITE LOCAL FIGHT: KAKAMPINK VS DDS

The Philippine version of this is easy to spot.

Kakampinks call their opponents “bobotantes,” “bayaran,” “enablers.” Marcos or Duterte supporters call critics “elitist,” “hypocrites,” “anti-Filipino.” 

Curiosity is rare. 
Real listening is rare. 

Most of the time, it’s just insults wearing different colors.

Researchers who studied the 2022 elections describe a moral gap. 

Each side sees itself as standing for decency and goodness, and the other side as not just mistaken but rotten. 

Labels like DDS, kakampink, Loyalist stopped being jokes. They became identities. 

People attached pride, shame, loyalty, and betrayal to them.

At the same time, analyses of Duterte’s rhetoric show a deliberate pattern: divide the world into allies and enemies, real citizens and threats, “us” and “them.” 

Once that picture settles, it becomes hard to imagine the other camp has any valid fears or reasons at all.

That is how false choices eat away at empathy. 

Not through a single big speech, but through thousands of small messages: we are good, they are bad. We are honest, they are corrupt. We love the country, they do not.

Once that story sticks, people stop wanting honest debate. They want their team to win.

🟥 WHY THIS THINKING IS SO WASTEFUL

Strip out the emotion for a second. Binary thinking isn’t just harsh. It’s wasteful.

Most real-world problems have more than one cause. 

Crime links to poverty, yes, but also to weak justice systems, local politics, broken families, drug markets, and lack of opportunity. 

Inflation is tied to global prices, local policy, supply chains, and sometimes pure bad luck. 

Migration is about money, safety, family, climate, and the search for a future.

If you insist there is only one cause—“because DDS,” “because dilawan,” “because communists,” “because oligarchs”—you will chase the wrong fix. 

You will hit one piece of the problem and leave the rest untouched. 

Research on policy failures repeats the same point: simple story, simple solution, disappointing outcome.

Back to the drug war. If you think the main issue is just “too many drug pushers,” then hunting pushers looks like the only answer. 

But public health and criminology work across countries shows that enforcement alone does not solve drug problems; treatment, prevention, and social support have to be part of it. 

Refuse to see those layers, and your “war” becomes endless, bloody, and shallow. Ultimately, useless. 

Binary thinking also forces people into defending a full bundle they never examined. 

If you are “pro this leader,” you feel pressure to defend every move. 

If you are “anti,” you feel pressure to reject every policy, even when it might help. 

At that point, you’re doing marketing, not analysis.

🟥 WHERE IT TURNS DISHONEST

Not everyone caught in this pattern is acting in bad faith. 

Many people never had any real training in critical thinking. 

Most media environments—from traditional TV to TikTok—reward speed, emotion, and hot takes. It’s easy to get dragged along.

But there is another layer: people who know better and push this anyway.

Influencers who turn every issue into “our side vs monsters.” 

Politicians who talk like they are at war 24/7. 

Commentators who never admit the other camp has a point because it might “confuse” the base. 

Propaganda researchers list false dichotomy as a standard tactic: push two options hard, ignore the rest, lace it with fear or anger, and people will line up.

At this point, “stupidity” is not the main problem. The problem is honesty.

There is also a useful idea here: mental flexibility. 

Call it the ability to adjust your view when new information comes in. 

Studies show that people with more of this flexibility tend to be less extreme and more open to actual conversation. 

People who lack it—who stay rigid—tend to see everything as a loyalty test.

When leaders and influencers keep selling “with us or against us,” again and again, that is not neutral or normal. 

It is a choice to keep people rigid and predictable, instead of helping them think.

🟥 WHAT THIS DOES TO DEMOCRACY

Zoom out.

Democracy can live with shouting matches. It cannot live with a public that sees the other half of the country as enemies to crush. 

In that climate, people start caring more about beating the other side than about fair rules or basic checks and balances.

Research on polarized democracies shows a clear pattern: once people are deeply locked into two hostile camps, they become more willing to excuse abuses of power from their own side, as long as those abuses hurt the “enemy.” 

They tolerate bending rules, attacking institutions, intimidating media, and even clear corruption, because the alternative is “letting the other side win.”

That is where strongmen like Duterte thrive. They don’t just ask you to vote for them. They tell you they are the only protection against the other tribe.  

So when we accept lines like “if you don’t support X, you don’t love your country,” the damage is bigger than a toxic thread.

We quietly weaken our own ability to hold any leader accountable, because we have convinced ourselves that letting “them” win is worse than letting “our” side break the rules.

That is the deal on the table: safety from the other camp, in exchange for looking away.

🟥 HOW TO PUSH BACK (IN SMALL, REAL WAYS)

So what do you do with all this if you are just trying to live your life and not lose your mind online?

A few habits help:

- Ask, “What’s missing?”  
 
Any time you hear “you’re either for this or against the country,” pause and ask: “What options are not being mentioned?” In most cases, there are more than two.

- Say the third position out loud.  

Use sentences like: “I care about safety and I care about human rights.” “I distrust corrupt politicians and I don’t think your favorite is the clean alternative.” That “and” is a quiet refusal to play the two-box game.

- Attack ideas, not worth.  

It is fine to say, “That policy is harmful,” or “That argument doesn’t add up.” Going straight to “people who think this are trash” is the line you cross before you start justifying worse behavior in the name of politics.

- Notice when you are being pushed emotionally.  

If a post makes you instantly furious or disgusted and then presents only two options, treat that as a red flag. False dichotomies often come wrapped in strong emotion exactly because emotion shuts down reflection.

These actions are small. They won’t fix the system on their own. But they train your mind not to hand over its agency so easily.

The way I see it, we are not as shallow as our feeds suggest. 

Most people can feel when something is too simple, when a story erases too many details, when a leader sounds a bit too sure. 

The instinct to question is still there. It just gets drowned out by noise and pressure to pick a jersey.

False dichotomies work only if we accept their starting point. The moment we refuse that, they lose power.  

The next time someone tells you you’re either with them or against them, the most honest answer is simple:

“Your question is too small for the real world.”

🟥 WHY I STARTED MORNING COFFEE THOUGHTS

When I started Morning Coffee Thoughts, this was the kind of thing I wanted to name and drag into the light. 

These habits sit quietly under our jokes, our debates, our family arguments. 

They shape how we talk about the country without us even noticing. 

I wanted to put a spotlight on these patterns one by one so we could actually see them, argue about them, and become more aware of how they work.

I’m not outside this problem. I fall into false dichotomies too. 

I catch myself picking teams in my head, or framing things as “smart vs stupid,” “good vs bad,” when I know the story is more complicated than that. 

The only difference is I try, as much as I can, to slow down, ground my reasoning, and keep some doors open in my head instead of slamming them shut.

That’s also why I try not to take sides in the usual way. 

For me, “pro-Philippines” isn’t a color or a last name. Being pro-Philippines means you care more about what actually works than about who gets the credit. 

It means you’re willing to hold uncomfortable, layered views because the country is uncomfortable and layered. 

If you are truly for the Philippines, you start to drift toward more careful reasoning, more context, more room for details that don’t fit the script. 

You stop needing the comfort of “our side always good, their side always bad,” because you’re aiming at something bigger than a camp: you’re aiming at what helps people live better, even when that answer cuts across the usual lines.

Thanks for joining me in this morning's reflection while having coffee. Magandang umaga. Sana masarap ang agahan mo.

Sara Duterte is about to hit the one-year mark of her impeachment "escape," and Congress is already telegraphing its next move: more noise, more drama, and very likely, more ways to protect her than to remove her. 

The real question is simpler — will they actually hold her accountable, or will this turn into another four-month delay procedural delay followed by a technicality kill?

Warning: Long piece ahead. 

🟥 HOW CONGRESS ALREADY LEARNED TO PROTECT SARA

Let's start with the dry run they already did in 2025.

On February 5 last year, the House pulled off something big on paper: 215 members signed the impeachment complaint against Sara Duterte — way above the one-third required. That's already the Articles of Impeachment under the Constitution; it should go straight to the Senate, straight to trial.

Instead, here's what happened:

The Senate received the articles but did not move "forthwith" as the Constitution says. Then–Senate President Chiz Escudero used the calendar like a shield — "priority bills," election season, not enough time.

The trial was pushed and pushed until June 10, four months after the House transmitted the complaint.

When they finally sat as an impeachment court, the first real move was a motion by Alan Peter Cayetano: send the articles back to the House "for certification" that the rules were followed.

The Senate voted 18–5 for that move. No evidence yet, no witnesses, no real hearing. Just a procedural U-turn.

Then the Supreme Court entered the scene. On July 25, 2025, it declared that impeachment unconstitutional, saying the one-year ban rule was violated because of earlier complaints filed in December 2024.

Finally, on August 6, 2025, the Senate voted 19–4 to archive the whole thing.

No acquittal. No verdict on the charges. Just a case killed by timing, technicalities, and institutional ping-pong.

That's the template. Everyone got to say, "We respect the Constitution." But the practical outcome was simple: protect Sara, delay accountability, and blame process for it.

Senator Risa Hontiveros called it what it was: "obstructions dressed up as protocol."

🟥 WHAT THEY'RE REALLY PROTECTING: THE CONFIDENTIAL FUNDS STORY

Why such effort to delay? To kill the impeacment?

Look at what's at stake.

The core allegations against Sara Duterte are not "simple politics." They revolve around money the public can't see — confidential funds worth hundreds of millions.

The numbers that keep coming up:

₱125 million in confidential funds for the Office of the Vice President in 2022, reportedly spent in 11 days.

Around ₱500 million more for DepEd in 2023, when she was also Education Secretary.

Together: ₱612.5 million in confidential funds that critics say remain poorly explained or improperly documented.

Commission on Audit reports and later investigations flagged red flags — questionable documentation, names that don't match civil registries, disbursements that don't line up clearly with actual operations.

The Ombudsman then ordered her to answer multiple complaints, and she filed a counter-affidavit last June 2025. Running parallel to the impeachment — separate but connected.

Here's the kicker: even the Supreme Court, when it killed the impeachment on technical grounds, made it clear it wasn't declaring her innocent. The ruling zeroed in on procedure and the one-year ban, not on clearing her name.

So when people like Terry Ridon say the basis for last year's impeachment "remains unchanged" for 2026, he's not being dramatic. The substance — the confidential funds — hasn't moved. What moved is the political ground around it.

🟥 THE SENATE AS SARA'S FIRST LINE OF DEFENSE

Now look at the Senate, because this is where conviction either happens or doesn't.

By design, it's easy to impeach in the House, hard to convict in the Senate:

House: one-third vote. That's 102 members; they reached more than double that last time.

Senate: two-thirds vote. That means 16 out of 24 senators need to say "guilty" to remove Sara from office.

So if you're Sara, you don't need 16 fans. You only need 9 senators who will not convict you. That's the blocking number.

After the 2025 midterms, Duterte-aligned candidates did well. Analysts expected maybe two pro-Duterte winners; they got around four. Bong Go topped the race; Bato dela Rosa placed third. Add in old allies like Imee Marcos, Robin Padilla, Rodante Marcoleta, Joel Villanueva, Jinggoy Estrada, and possibly Camille Villar and Alan Peter Cayetano, and you get a rough Duterte bloc in the Senate of around seven to nine people.

Many of them already showed their hand:

They backed the moves that delayed and eventually archived the impeachment in 2025.

They publicly defended Sara or attacked the impeachment drive as politically motivated.

With that bloc in place, the math tilts:

A solid 7–9 pro-Sara senators already give her a path to avoid conviction, even if a trial actually happens.

And that's before you factor in "swing" senators who might not be pro-Duterte, but are cautious about 2028, their own alliances, and possible backlash.

Now layer on top the leadership shift:

Francis Escudero, who oversaw the slow-walk of 2025, is no longer Senate President. Vicente "Tito" Sotto is back, and he's saying all the right things — that the Senate will act "forthwith," that they'll do their duty if a new case arrives.

Sounds good. But we already know the trick: "acting forthwith" doesn't automatically mean "acting toward a full trial." It could also mean:

convene quickly,

vote quickly on a motion to return the articles to the House again, or

vote quickly to archive, citing the unresolved Supreme Court ruling and "legal uncertainty."

So yes, different Senate President, different rhetoric, same leverage: numbers that favor obstruction.

The August 2025 vote to archive showed this clearly: 19 senators voted to archive (including the entire Duterte bloc plus several majority members), while only four voted against — Sotto, Bam Aquino, Risa Hontiveros, and Francis Pangilinan. Panfilo Lacson abstained.

🟥 THE SUPREME COURT: HOW LEGAL GREY AREAS BECOME POLITICAL WEAPONS

The Supreme Court's 2025 ruling didn't just kill one impeachment. It created a new playbook for how to block the next one.

A few key moves in that decision:

The Court said the one-year ban on filing impeachment cases was violated because of earlier complaints against Sara filed in December 2024.

It treated those earlier complaints as "terminated" in a way that made the February 5, 2025 House action unconstitutional.

It also criticized parts of the House process — essentially saying due process wasn't fully observed when the complaint was endorsed and transmitted.

The House filed a motion for reconsideration in August 2025, challenging both the facts and the legal reasoning. As of early January 2026, that motion for reconsideration remains pending. No clarity, no final word.

Opposition groups like 1Sambayan have challenged the Court's factual findings, arguing the timeline the Court used was "demonstrably false" — that the fourth complaint was actually filed and voted on before the first three were archived on February 5, 2025.

Here's where that helps obstruction:

Any ally of Sara can argue that Congress should wait for the Supreme Court's final word before moving on a new impeachment.

If Congress pushes through anyway, they can run back to the Court to ask for temporary restraining orders or new rulings, saying the constitutional rules around impeachment are still unclear.

Even the threat of intervention can slow down the Senate, which already showed in 2025 that it's more than willing to let the Court "take the lead" on a political question.

So the Court doesn't need to say, "We defend Sara." All it needs to do is sit on the motion for reconsideration, keep legal questions hanging, and entertain petitions. Slowness itself becomes a form of protection.

🟥 THE HOUSE: HAS THE NUMBERS, BUT NOT THE NERVE

In the House, the story is less about capability and more about courage.

Capability is clear:

They've done it once. 215 signatures initially, then 240 lawmakers backing the impeachment by the time it reached the Senate in 2025.

The Marcos camp still controls a supermajority in the House — 240 of 310 members. They can gather one-third again if they really want to.

But the mood now is more cautious. Several new threads:

The Makabayan bloc (ACT Teachers Rep. Antonio Tinio, Kabataan Rep. Raoul Manuel, among others) and some of the same groups that filed earlier complaints are already prepping to endorse a fresh impeachment once the one-year ban expires around February 6, 2026.

Ridon and others keep hammering the confidential funds angle — that nothing about the alleged misuse has been resolved, only the technical case has. Ridon said: "The rationale for last year's impeachment remains unchanged for 2026."

Yet coverage in the last few days says a lot of House members are not exactly rushing to sign anything. A Philippine Star report on January 14, 2026 frames it bluntly: "Renewed impeachment proceedings against Vice President Sara Duterte may face stiffer resistance in both chambers." Senator Sherwin Gatchalian said support for Sara has grown stronger.

Instead, we're hearing:

- talk about "focusing on other priorities,"

- hints that they don't want impeachment tied to the 2026 budget or used as some kind of bargaining chip — Senator Imee Marcos claimed the 2026 budget was being used to incentivize impeachment, though Ridon denied this,

- and the Palace publicly saying Marcos won't oppose impeachment, but also won't lead the charge. Palace officials say it's about "accountability," not absolution.

So the House is in a weird place:

They're the only ones who can restart the process. They also know the Senate can quietly kill it again. And they're watching 2028 already, asking: is picking this fight worth it if the endgame is still a failed conviction?

🟥 THE GHOST OF 2028 HANGING OVER EVERYTHING

You can't talk about Sara's impeachment without talking about 2028.

Right now:

Pulse Asia surveys from December 2025 show Sara Duterte with 56% approval and 54% trust ratings nationwide, while Marcos is at 34% approval and 32% trust — negative territory.

She remains a front-runner for 2028, especially with Marcos term-limited and the dynasties openly at war.

Conviction in an impeachment case means disqualification from public office — perpetual ban. That's the death blow to any presidential ambition.

So think about how each faction in Congress looks at impeachment:

Pro-Duterte senators: They're not just defending her. They're protecting their future under a possible President Sara.

Marcos allies: Removing Sara by impeachment would clear the 2028 field — but it also looks like using impeachment as a weapon, which can backfire if voters see it as pure political power play.

Everyone else: They're counting whether tying themselves to either side helps or hurts them when it's their turn to face voters.

This is why impeachment keeps getting treated less like a question of "guilty or not guilty" and more like a chess piece with a four-year horizon.

If she's removed, who gets stronger? If she survives, who gets weaker?

The Marcos-Duterte alliance — what analysts called a "mega-dynasty" marriage — has completely collapsed into what observers describe as a "scorched-earth" war that will intensify through 2028.

🟥 WHAT OBSTRUCTION WILL LOOK LIKE IF A NEW CASE IS FILED

If someone files a new impeachment complaint after the one-year bar lapses on February 6, 2026, it won't look like "obstruction" on the surface. It will look like "concern for process" and "respect for institutions."

You'll likely see:

In the House:

Long, dramatic hearings on "sufficiency in form and substance," even if everyone already knows the content of the complaints.

Arguments that they should wait for the Supreme Court to resolve the motion for reconsideration on the 2025 ruling before moving decisively.

Representatives saying lines like "we must avoid weaponizing impeachment," while doing the math on whether Marcos or Sara will be stronger by 2028. Navotas Rep. Toby Tiangco has already said he won't support a new complaint unless "big fish" in the flood control scam are also hit — conditions first before accountability.

In the Senate:

A promise to act "forthwith," turned into quick procedural moves — motions to return, motions to clarify, motions to archive.

Citing the Supreme Court everywhere: "We must align with the Court's precedent," "we cannot repeat constitutional mistakes."

Duterte-bloc senators reminding everyone that impeachment "should not be used as a political weapon," even as they use every political tool to block conviction.

In the Supreme Court:

New petitions claiming the fresh complaint violates some aspect of the 2025 ruling or the one-year rule.

No rush to decide on the motion for reconsideration, because uncertainty actually serves everyone who doesn't want a clean, straightforward trial.

It's obstruction in a barong. All protocol, all respectability, all "we're just following the law" — but the net effect is still to keep Sara in place.

🟥 WHAT THIS SAYS ABOUT PHILIPPINE DEMOCRACY

The ugliest part of this story is how easily alleged abuses can slip past real accountability, and how the institutions around them react as if that’s normal.

Look at the bigger pattern:

Political dynasties dominate Congress — around 80% of lawmakers have family ties in politics, the highest rate in Asia and possibly the world.

That same Congress refuses to pass any real anti-dynasty law, even though the Constitution has been asking for one since 1987 and surveys show 54% of Filipinos want it. 

Cardinal Pablo Virgilio David warned in January 2026 that Congress's failure to act could "ignite public outrage," but noted Congress is "dominated by the very same political dynasties."

When impeachment is used — Erap in 2001, Chief Justice Corona in 2012 — it tends to happen only when enough of the elite has decided someone is expendable, and when public pressure is massive.

President Joseph Estrada was impeached in November 2000, with articles transmitted the same day. His trial began just 24 days later on December 7, 2000. 

When senators voted 11-10 not to open evidence, prosecutors walked out and EDSA II forced his resignation in January 2001. The trial lasted only 23 days total.

Chief Justice Renato Corona was impeached December 12, 2011, with his Senate trial beginning January 16, 2012 — just 35 days later. After 134 days of hearings, senators voted 20-3 to convict him on May 29, 2012 for failing to disclose his assets. He became the first official removed from office through impeachment.

Sara's case is different. She's not expendable to her network. And while there's outrage, it hasn't reached the same level of street pressure. 

So what we see instead is impeachment being used as leverage in a dynastic war — not as a clear instrument of public accountability.

The confidential funds issue should be simple:

If you mishandled the people's money, especially funds shielded from normal transparency, you answer for it.

But in a dynasty-run Congress, the instinct is:

If we let one of us fall like that, what stops them from using the same tool on us later?

That's the real obstruction. Not just rules. Not just Chiz or Sotto or the Supreme Court. It's a class instinct — protect the powerful, let technicalities kill cases, and hope the public gets tired or distracted.

The Center for Media Freedom and Responsibility concluded: "The Senate has effectively cast the impeachment complaint in a virtual limbo. Accountability has been put on hold and the long watch begins."

🟥 THE BOTTOM LINE

If a new impeachment against Sara Duterte happens this year, it will be sold as a test of her. But it's really a test of us.

Do we accept a system where 612.5 million in confidential funds can swirl around with no real consequences, as long as the right families hold the right numbers in the Senate?

Or do we start seeing every delay, every technicality, every "respect for process" speech for what it is — a choice to keep power safe from accountability?

That's the question that will outlast this Congress, this impeachment, and maybe even 2028.

🟥 Sources:

Check the comment section.

🟥 Disclaimer: 

The image used for this piece shows a sword hanging above Sara Duterte’s head. This is a reference to the “Sword of Damocles,” an old story used to symbolize the constant danger or risk hanging over someone in power. It does not depict or endorse violence, harm, or a literal threat against her or any person. It is a metaphor for political and legal peril, not a call to action.

When I line these stories up in my head, they stop looking like separate headlines.

The impeachment of Sara Duterte and that plunder case.  

The talk of impeaching Bongbong Marcos.  

A colonel publicly withdrawing his support.  

The rumored recantation of Alcantara and the BGC Boys.  

I keep asking myself: am I just being paranoid, or is this all part of the same script?

🟥 LAYING IT OUT AS I SEE IT

First, the things that are not rumor.

Sara Duterte is facing plunder and related complaints over the missing P612.5 million in confidential funds, with civil society and church groups saying the funds were misused and the paper trail is full of sketchy receipts and fake-sounding payees.  

The earlier impeachment attempt against her was junked by the Supreme Court because it violated the one‑year rule, but that one‑year bar lapses in early February 2026, and people in Congress are already talking about filing a new complaint.

On the other side, there is noise about impeaching Marcos himself. Some groups allegedly approached opposition figures about an impeachment case, and the Palace went on record to call it unsubstantiated and political maneuvering.

Then there is the military angle. An Army colonel publicly announced he was withdrawing his support from Marcos as Commander‑in‑Chief, and he was relieved and placed under investigation. Within days, he voluntarily returned to military control and is now cooperating with the ongoing investigation. 

The AFP says it is looking into whether anyone else influenced him. 

Whatever the official line is, that kind of break in ranks—even a temporary one—is not normal.

And in the middle of the flood control scandal, the DOJ is very clear on paper: Henry Alcantara has not officially recanted his testimony. No affidavit, no sworn retraction. 

At the same time, Alcantara and the so‑called BGC Boys are facing tax and corruption exposure after being linked to billions in casino cash conversions and alleged laundering of money tied to ghost flood control projects.

🟥 THE RUMOR LAYER I CAN'T IGNORE

On top of that, there is the thing that keeps getting repeated: the supposed plan for Alcantara and the BGC Boys to recant, or at least soften, their stories.

The version that keeps surfacing is simple and dirty: help Sara survive impeachment now, and in return, hope for executive clemency later if she becomes president in 2028.

There is no court document that proves that deal exists. But it has been loud enough that Malacañang itself felt the need to publicly warn the BGC Boys not to let themselves be used or fooled. 

That, to me, is already a signal that these "talks" are not just floating in some tiny rumor bubble. 

And now Senator Erwin Tulfo has announced that the Senate Blue Ribbon Committee will investigate the alleged plans of Alcantara and the BGC Boys to recant their testimonies. 

The hearing is scheduled for January 19, 2026. The fact that the Senate thinks this is worth investigating officially suggests the rumor has legs.

So on one hand, I have the DOJ saying: no recantation. 

On the other hand, I keep seeing politicians, columnists, and official spokespeople reacting to the possibility of a recantation. 

And now the Senate is setting up a formal investigation into whether it is happening. 

That gap—between what is documented and what is being investigated—is exactly where Philippine politics tends to operate.

🟥 THE INCENTIVES THAT MAKE THIS FEEL CONNECTED

When I strip it down to motives, it starts making sense in a way I almost wish it didn't.

Sara needs survival. A renewed impeachment complaint that prospers in the Senate does not just remove her from office, it bars her from holding public office again. That would kill any chance of a 2028 presidential run. 

Coupled with plunder complaints over confidential funds, that is a serious threat to her political future.

Marcos needs control. The flood control scandal has already exposed budget insertions, ghost projects, and testimonies that point toward lawmakers and officials, with some accusations creeping closer to his circle. 

Even if an impeachment case against him is still talk at this stage, the fact that it is being floated at all dents his already unstable image and puts his camp on permanent defense.

Alcantara and the BGC Boys need a way out. They are looking at plunder, tax, and corruption cases. 

Alcantara has already paid back large amounts and lost his engineering license. 

People in that position either double down as state witnesses, or they look for powerful patrons who might someday sign a piece of paper that gets them out.

The military, for its part, clearly does not want to be dragged into open political crisis. 

But an officer publicly breaking with the President shows there is unease inside the institution. 

The leadership is trying to ring‑fence it as an isolated incident, and in Mongao's case, they appear to have contained it after he returned to military control. 

Whether it stays contained depends on how ugly this political war gets and whether other officers feel emboldened to speak out.

When I look at all that, it starts to feel less like random chaos and more like several players trying to survive the same coming storm: 2028.

🟥 CONNECTED, OR JUST MY BRAIN DRAWING LINES?

If I answer like a lawyer, I have to say: these are still separate cases. Separate dockets. Separate facts. A plunder complaint here, an impeachment talk there, a military investigation in another corner, and a DOJ statement about a non‑existent recantation.

But I am not a lawyer. I am a person watching patterns.

I see the same flood control scandal being used as ammunition by different sides. 

I see the same set of witnesses becoming central to multiple power struggles. 

I see the same Senate that may try Sara also being the arena where any serious case against Marcos would end up. 

I see dynasties with overlapping legal risks and overlapping timelines circling 2028 like it is the last lifeboat.

So my honest answer to myself is this:

No, there is no single public document that proves one grand conspiracy tying together Sara's plunder case, Marcos' impeachment chatter, the colonel's withdrawal of support, and the rumored recantation of Alcantara and the BGC Boys.

But yes, the incentives of everyone involved are aligned in a way that makes coordination, pressure, and quiet deals not just possible, but logical in the way Philippine politics usually works.

🟥 WHERE THAT LEAVES ME

I don't think it is "dirty imagination" to see a pattern here.

It feels more like refusing to pretend that each headline lives in its own little box. 

Sara's possible impeachment, Marcos' vulnerability, the nervousness inside the military, and the value of witnesses like Alcantara and the BGC Boys all sit inside one shared ecosystem: a political class fighting over power, protection, and 2028.

I might be wrong about the exact deals. I might be wrong about who promised what to whom. But I do not think I am wrong to say this:

Too many powerful people right now are desperate not to be the one left holding the bag when the music stops.
